items.myth: Rebase is dangerous and should be avoided.
items.reality: Rebase is *only* dangerous on shared branches. On your private branch — to sync with `main` or clean up your commits — it's safe and produces history reviewers love.
items.example:
```bash
# Safe & encouraged:
git switch feature/my-private
git rebase origin/main
```

items.myth: Merge and rebase produce different final code.
items.reality: They produce the **same final code** if conflicts are resolved consistently. What differs is the *history graph* — merge keeps both branches visible; rebase makes one line.
items.example:
```bash
# Same end state, different log:
#   merge: M (two parents)
#   rebase: A'-B'-C' linear
```

items.myth: `git pull` is a single atomic operation.
items.reality: `git pull` is `git fetch` followed by `git merge` (or `git rebase` if configured). When it goes wrong, troubleshoot by splitting them.
items.example:
```bash
git fetch origin            # get updates
git log HEAD..origin/main   # see what's new
git merge origin/main        # or rebase
```

items.myth: Squash-merge is the same as a rebase.
items.reality: **Squash-merge** flattens *all* feature commits into a *single* new commit on `main` — losing the per-commit granularity. **Rebase** preserves each commit (with new SHAs) as a linear sequence on top of `main`.
items.example:
```
feature: A-B-C
rebase  main+main+A'+B'+C'
squash  main+S (S has all of A+B+C content but one message)
```

items.reality: It IS a merge (it merges your branch in) — it just doesn't need to *create* a merge commit because the branch hasn't diverged. Use `--no-ff` if you want the merge commit anyway as a marker.
items.example:
```bash
git merge feature/auth          # may fast-forward
git merge --no-ff feature/auth   # always creates a merge commit
```
